stem: How does a slice-selection gradient localize RF excitation?

options.text: It makes every position precess at the same frequency
It suppresses transverse magnetization outside the receiver bandwidth after readout
It makes Larmor frequency vary with position so the RF bandwidth excites a defined spatial range
It converts phase differences directly into image brightness

explanation: During the slice gradient, position along the gradient corresponds to resonance frequency. The RF pulse excites only spins whose frequencies fall inside its transmitted bandwidth.

objective: Predict slice thickness from RF bandwidth.
stem: At a fixed slice-selection gradient strength, what is the effect of increasing RF pulse bandwidth?

options.text: The selected slice becomes thinner
The slice moves without changing thickness
The slice thickness becomes independent of frequency
The selected slice becomes thicker

explanation: A wider transmitted frequency range covers a larger position range when the frequency-versus-position slope is fixed. The result is a thicker excited slice.

objective: Predict slice thickness from gradient strength.
stem: At a fixed RF bandwidth, what is the effect of increasing the slice-selection gradient strength?

options.text: The selected slice becomes thinner
The selected slice becomes thicker
The slice shifts to isocenter without changing thickness
The RF pulse excites the entire imaging volume

explanation: A stronger gradient creates a steeper frequency change per unit distance. The same RF frequency range therefore spans a smaller distance and selects a thinner slice.

objective: Relate center frequency to slice position.
stem: With gradient strength and RF bandwidth unchanged, how is a different parallel slice selected?

options.text: The receiver bandwidth is set to zero
The center frequency of the RF pulse is changed
The phase-encoding matrix is doubled
The echo time is lengthened

explanation: Changing RF center frequency targets another position on the slice gradient's frequency map. Gradient amplitude and RF bandwidth chiefly determine the frequency-to-position slope and slice thickness.

objective: Identify the usual axial slice gradient.
stem: For a conventional axial acquisition, which physical gradient axis commonly performs slice selection?

options.text: The x gradient only
The y gradient only
The z gradient
No gradient is required

explanation: The z-axis runs along the bore and is perpendicular to a standard axial plane, so the z gradient commonly selects axial slices. Oblique imaging combines gradient axes as needed.
